title: Strong coupling between quantized magnon modes in a YIG microstucture and microwaves in a superconducting resonator
authors: Seth W. Kurfman, Philipp Geyer, Anoop Kamalasanan, Karl Heimrich, Kwangyul Hu, Paul Tharnier, Frank Heyroth, Michael Flatté, Georg Schmidt
abstract: Strong-coupling experiments based on magnons enable the exploration into on-chip demonstrations involving numerous long-lived excitations. Yttrium iron garnet (YIG) has been considered for decades as a gold standard material for magnonics due to its low-loss magnonic properties. While YIG has successfully demonstrated strong-coupling in macroscopic device geometries, the strong coupling of magnons in truly sub-10 micron YIG structures to date has not yet been realized. This obstacle is due to the difficulty producing large enough effective magnonic mode volume necessary primarily due to thickness limitations of YIG deposition and device fabrication techniques. Here, we demonstrate the use of a microplatelet of YIG, manufactured from a single crystal of YIG via focused ion beam (FIB) techniques, placed on a constricted inductive line of an optimized superconducting lumped element LC resonator to achieve strong coupling between numerous magnon modes and the LC resonator photons. These experimental findings are qualitatively backed by micromagnetic simulations and quantitatively supported by analytical calculations to identify the magnon modes corresponding to the experimentally observed anti-crossings in the microwave transmission signal. Further, we show that these anti-crossings remain even at incredibly low device input powers ($\leq 10$ fW). The fabrication techniques and device geometry enable the deterministic use of numerous confined magnon modes in micron-scale YIG structures for various magnetic field strengths and orientations at substantially reduced device powers. The results here establish a foundational path forward to achieving efficient magnon-based strong-coupling experiments in micron-scale YIG magnetic elements for effective on-chip studies.
summary: The study achieves strong coupling between multiple magnon modes and superconducting resonator photons in micron-scale YIG structures. By using FIB-milled microplatelets, the researchers overcame previous fabrication limits on mode volume. This provides a foundational method for efficient, low-power, on-chip magnonic quantum technologies.
main_problem: Achieving strong coupling between magnons and microwave photons in sub-10 micron Yttrium Iron Garnet (YIG) structures has been difficult due to limited mode volume caused by YIG thickness and fabrication constraints.
main_result: The researchers demonstrated strong coupling between numerous magnon modes in a FIB-manufactured YIG microplatelet and a superconducting LC resonator, even at extremely low input powers (<= 10 fW).
method: The team used Focused Ion Beam (FIB) techniques to create a YIG microplatelet from a single crystal and placed it on a constricted inductive line of an optimized superconducting lumped element LC resonator.
model_or_system: The system consists of quantized magnon modes within a micron-scale YIG microplatelet coupled to photons in a superconducting lumped element LC resonator.
key_observables: Microwave transmission signal and anti-crossings in the transmission spectrum.
important_parameters: Sub-10 micron YIG structures, device input powers <= 10 fW.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the challenge of small-scale magnonics, describes the FIB fabrication and resonator design, presents experimental evidence of anti-crossings, validates results with micromagnetic simulations and analytical calculations, and demonstrates low-power operation.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and open quantum systems as it demonstrates a platform for scalable, on-chip strong coupling between many long-lived excitations (magnons) and microwave photons, essential for hybrid quantum architectures.
analysis_basis: abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable

Spintronics.note: The paper is a direct match as it demonstrates the strong coupling between quantized magnon modes and superconducting cavity photons, which is a central theme of the optics-spintronics interface.
